mnemonics.title: Five Objectives of Distributed Systems
mnemonics.content: ROCCS - Resource Sharing, Openness, Concurrency, Scalability, Security (Fault Tolerance) - Remember: 'Ruthless Otters Catch Crazy Salmon'

mnemonics.title: Transparency Types
mnemonics.content: ALMRAF - Access, Location, Migration, Replication, Availability (Concurrency), Failure - Remember: 'Always Look Main Replicas Available For Use'

mnemonics.title: Distributed System Challenges
mnemonics.content: HSSCSF - Heterogeneity, Security, Scalability, Synchronization, Concurrency, Fault tolerance - Remember: 'Hackers Steal Scalable Synchronized Concurrent Code Frequently'

mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP - Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance - During Partition, you must CHOOSE - remember: 'Can't Always Please' (during partitions)

acronyms.usage: Allows calling functions on remote systems as if they were local - like making a phone call to another office
Java's version of RPC for calling methods on remote objects - like having a representative in another office do work for you
Distributed file system designed for large-scale data processing - demonstrates replication and fault tolerance
Open-source implementation of GFS - used in Big Data processing clusters
Geographically distributed servers for faster content delivery - reduces latency for global users

visualTips.visualization: Draw a pyramid: Bottom = Hardware/Network, Middle = Middleware, Top = Applications/Services. Show arrows of communication between layers
Draw a triangle with C, A, P at that during vertices. Show Normal operation all three work, but during Partition (P), must choose either C or A (line cuts across)
Draw client on left, server on right, with request arrow going right (numbered 1), response arrow going left (numbered 2). Show middleware layer below as bridge
Vertical: Stack more resources on single box (like layers). Horizontal: Add more boxes side-by-side (like extending a line of servers)

analogies.analogy: Kitchen (servers) prepares food, Waiters (network) deliver to Tables (clients). Each waiter doesn't need to know how food is cooked, just delivers. If one chef gets sick, others cover - that's fault tolerance.

Middleware is like a simultaneous translator at a UN meeting. It sits between speakers (applications) who speak different languages (use different systems) and ensures everyone understands each other.
Keeping copies of important documents in different locations. If one office burns down, you still have copies. This is how distributed systems handle disk failures.
A load balancer distributes cars (requests) across multiple lanes (servers). Prevents one lane from being jammed while others are empty - like a smart intersection.
